coredata.dc:title: DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS polymer/ZnO nanorods Schottky junction ultraviolet photodetector: Photoresponse, external quantum efficiency, detectivity, and responsivity augmentation using N doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Dhar, Saurab
Majumder, Tanmoy
Chakraborty, Pinak
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description:
Schottky junction ultraviolet (UV) photodetector was fabricated by spin coating a hole conducting polymer, poly 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene: polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) on hydrothermally grown zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorod array. The photodetector performance was improved by increasing the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) additive. The UV detector performance was further enhanced significantly by sensitizing ZnO nanorods with N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/DMSO-PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction device demonstrated superior external quantum efficiency (EQE ∼ 90063%), responsivity (
R λ
∼247 A/W) and detectivity (
D λ
∼2.42 × 1011 Hz1/2/W) at 340 nm wavelength and –1V external bias. The EQE of NGQD modified sample was 56 times higher than pristine PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NR and 4.3 times higher than DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NRs device. NGQD sensitized detector showed superior photocurrent of 80.77 mA/cm2 at 340 nm wavelength, –1V external bias, which was 2 times higher than DMSO modified and 32 times higher than pristine PEDOT:PSS based device. The photocurrent rise and decay time of NGQD sensitized NRs are very fast compared to other photodetectors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanorods
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
PEDOT:PSS
Schottky junction UV detector
External quantum efficiency (EQE)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of carrier transport in PEDOT:PSS polymer. (a) unmodified PEDOT:PSS, conductive PEDOT grains (blue) are surrounded by an insulating PSS shell (green) (b) thinning of PSS layer after addition of DMSO solvent, which creates better carrier transport. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

STM current images of (a) pristine and (b) DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS (3:4 vol ratio) with STM bias voltage 0.5V and amplitude setpoint 357.79 mV, (c) typical STM I-V curve characteristics of pristine PEDOT:PSS film. Inset figures represent the schematic representation of STM imaging and tunneling mechanism between tip and the polymer and (d) Combined STM I-V plots of pristine and DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS films (3:4 vol ratio). Tunneling conductance
(
d I / d V
)
vs V plots are shown at inset.

Top view SEM micrographs of (a) ZnO nanorods grown on FTO coated glass substrate, (b) DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS(3:4 vol ratio) deposited on ZnO nanorods and (c) schematic device structure of a UV photodetector device. The inset photographs in Fig. (b) demonstrates the transparency of the ZnO NR samples coated with untreated PEDOT:PSS and DMSO treated PEDOT:PSS.

(a) AFM topology of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots deposited on p-Si substrate, (b) plane view TEM micrograph of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), (c) SEM micrograph of NGQD sensitized ZnO nanorods with EDX imaging and (d) Elemental mapping of O, Zn, C and N.

(a) Semi-logarithmic J–V characteristics of S2 and S3 device at dark and under illumination of white light of intensity 80 mW/cm2. Semi-logarithmic J–V characteristics of PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NRs Schottky junction (S1) in dark and white light (inset), (b) transit photocurrent (J-t) characteristics for S1, S2 and S3 devices under broadband light source of illumination intensity 80 mW/cm2 at −1 V bias voltage.
External quantum efficiency (EQE) vs wavelength plots of S1, S2 and S3 samples under the reverse bias −1 V.

(a) Typical UV–visible absorption spectrum of NGQD solution, (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnO nanorods before and after NGQD attachment and after DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS coating. Schematic representation of the carrier transport mechanism of S3 device is shown at the inset.

(a) Transient photoresponse of S1, S2 and S3 devices measured under ON/OFF UV light of wavelength 340 nm with power density 0.38 mW/cm−2 at -1V bias voltage, (b) Experimental (open circle) and fitted curves (solid line) of the photocurrent rise and decay response of the sample S3.
(a) Responsivity vs wavelength and (b) Detectivity vs wavelength plots of S1, S2 and S3 photodetectors measured at -1V bias.
Fitted results of photocurrent rise and decay time constants for S1, S2 and S3 photodetectors.
Comparative UV sensing performance of different ZnO/PEDOT:PSS-based device structures.

head.ce:title: DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS polymer/ZnO nanorods Schottky junction ultraviolet photodetector: Photoresponse, external quantum efficiency, detectivity, and responsivity augmentation using N doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala, 799046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Schottky junction ultraviolet (UV) photodetector was fabricated by spin coating a hole conducting polymer, poly 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene: polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) on hydrothermally grown zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorod array. The photodetector performance was improved by increasing the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) additive. The UV detector performance was further enhanced significantly by sensitizing ZnO nanorods with N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/DMSO-PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction device demonstrated superior external quantum efficiency (EQE ∼ 90063%), responsivity (
R λ
∼247 A/W) and detectivity (
D λ
∼2.42 × 1011 Hz1/2/W) at 340 nm wavelength and –1V external bias. The EQE of NGQD modified sample was 56 times higher than pristine PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NR and 4.3 times higher than DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NRs device. NGQD sensitized detector showed superior photocurrent of 80.77 mA/cm2 at 340 nm wavelength, –1V external bias, which was 2 times higher than DMSO modified and 32 times higher than pristine PEDOT:PSS based device. The photocurrent rise and decay time of NGQD sensitized NRs are very fast compared to other photodetectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Schottky junction photodetectors are attractive due to their simple device geometry, fast response and recovery time, low dark current and high quantum efficiency. [1–6] Such kind of photodetectors has been fabricated by depositing novel metal electrodes with larger work function (φ ∼ 4.2 to 6.32eV) such as Au, Ag, Pt, Pd on p or n-type semiconductors [7,8]. Recently, it has been also observed that monolayer graphene film could be an attractive material in order to get Schottky junction for photodetector application [9,10]. Metal/n-type ZnO semiconductor Schottky junctions have been extensively studied for ultraviolet (UV) photodetector applications due to its wide optical band gap (3.2–3.4 eV), easy synthesis process, high gain and overall environmental friendly nature [7,8,11–13]. However, fabrication of metal-semiconductor contacts usually needs high vacuum techniques and several lithographic steps, which raise the fabrication cost of the device. On the other hand, conducting polymer based electrodes are attractive due to its easy and low cost deposition process [14–18]. More importantly, such kinds of polymer based electrodes have potential applications in flexible and wearable electronics [19,20]. Poly 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene: polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) is a hole conducting conjugated polymer has been widely investigated in organic solar cells [21,22], light emitting diodes [23,24], field effect transistors [25,26], and photodetectors [16,27]. Nowadays, PEDOT:PSS has been utilized as an alternative material for transparent conducting electrodes due to its superior carrier mobility, large work function (5–5.2 eV) and excellent transparency (above 90%) in a wide spectral range (250 nm–800 nm) [16,20]. However, the electrical conductivity (σ) of pristine PEDOT:PSS films is very poor (σ ∼ 0.5 to 1 S/cm) [28] compared to conventional transparent conducting oxides (TCO) or metal electrodes (3300 to 10,000 S/cm) [29]. Several research works have been carried out to improve the electrical conductivity and transparency of PEDOT:PSS films by acid treatment or doping with various organic solvents. Xia et al. [28] prepared high conductive PEDOT:PSS films of maximum conductivity∼ 3065 S/cm by treating with concentrated H2SO4 and utilized as transparent conducting electrodes in polymer solar cell. Yeon et al. [30] improved the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS up to 4100 S/cm by using HNO3 treatment. Such high conductive films were used as the counter electrodes for dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) and they have achieved power conversion efficiency ∼8.59%. Ouyang et al. [31] reported the improvement of electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer using organic solvents such as ethylene glycol (EG), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and sorbitol. The maximum conductivity of the films was obtained σ∼ 1057 S/cm after DMSO doping [32].

Recently, UV photodetectors using n-type ZnO/PEDOT:PSS conducting polymer based Schottky junction have been attracted much attention due to its high sensitivity, low cost and easy fabrication process [16,17]. But the poor electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer decreases the carrier collection, which gives rise to slower photoresponse and lowering device performance.

On the other hand, attachment of semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdS, CdSe, CdTe, PbS) with ZnO nanorods also improve the photoconductivity and suppress carrier recombination of the photodetectors [27,33,34]. However, most of the Quantum dots (QDs) are highly toxic and may create many health related issues [35,36]. Recently, the photoconductive properties of ZnO nanorods have been enhanced several times by sensitizing with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [37,38]. These carbon nanomaterials have been utilized instead of semiconductor QDs due to their size tuneable optical absorption, emission properties and favourable band alignment [37,39,40]. More importantly, such new kinds of QDs are made from most abundant and nontoxic elements on earth with minimal low synthesis cost [41,42]. Doping with several elements like nitrogen, sulphur, boron etc. strengthen optical absorption of GQDs from ultraviolet to visible range [43–45].

In our previous report, we have studied ZnO nanorod/PEDOT:PSS based Schottky junction UV detectors with high external quantum efficiency (EQE∼1617%), detectivity (D
λ ∼3 × 1011Hz1/2/W) and responsivity (R
λ ∼4.4A/W) [46]. The photodetector performance was further enhanced after sensitizing the nanorods with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which has strong UV photoabsorption (EQE∼13167%, D
λ ∼ 1.29 × 1012 Hz1/2/W and R
λ ∼36A/W). However, in ZnO nanorod/PEDOT:PSS junction, the poor electrical conductivity of pristine PEDOT:PSS polymer increases interfacial resistance and decreases the carrier collection, which give rise to slower photoresponse and lowering device performance. The detector performance of the Schottky junction device could be improved further by increasing the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer. Simultaneously, sensitization of ZnO NRs with doped GQDs can also boost the photoabsorption and increase photogenerated carriers.

In this article, we have improved the UV photoresponse properties of ZnO Nanorods/PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction by enhancing the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer using DMSO solvent. The conductivity enhancement of PEDOT:PSS polymer layer has been verified by four probe method along with STM analysis. The UV photodetector performance was further enhanced by attaching nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as UV sensitizer on ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs) surface. The key photodetector parameters such as EQE, detectivity, responsivity, response and recovery time have been studied in details.

ZnO NRs were synthesized hydrothermally on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates. The detailed growth procedure has been reported elsewhere [46,47]. Prior to the growth of ZnO NRs, patterned FTO coated glass substrates were ultrasonically cleaned in DI water, followed by acetone and isopropyl alcohol. ZnO nanoparticle seed layer solution was prepared by adding 0.01M of zinc acetate dehydrate (Merck 99%) in 50 ml isopropyl alcohol (Merck 99%) followed by heating and stirring at 65OC for 1 h. Afterwards, 0.02M of diethanolamine (Merck 99.5%) was added to the above solution. The solution was cooled to room temperature and spin coated on the cleaned FTO coated glass substrate followed by annealing at 200O C for 1hr. Nanorods were grown by dipping the seeded substrates in a Teflon lined stainless steel hydrothermal reactor, containing zinc nitrate (0.05M) (Sigma Aldrich 98%) and HMTA (0.05M) (Sigma Aldrich 99%) solutions. The reaction temperature of the solution was maintained at 90OC for 4 h. The grown nanorods were cleaned with DI water and annealed at 400OC for 2 h for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis NGQDs were reported elsewhere [37,39]. In brief, 1 mM of citric acid, and 3 mM of urea were dissolved in DI water by vigorously stirring for few minutes. Afterwards, the solution was kept in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated up to 180OC for 8 h followed by cooling to room temperature. The heating and cooling rate was precisely regulated at 2 °C/min. The as-grown NGQDs were collected by centrifuging the solutions at 9000 rpm for 30 min and redispersed in DI water for further use. NGQDs were attached by dipping the ZnO NR samples into the NGQDs solution for 2 h duration.

The photodetector devices were fabricated by spin coating of PEDOT:PSS polymer solutions (Sigma Aldrich, conducting grade 1.3 wt % dispersion in H2O with PEDOT content 0.5 wt % and PSS content, 0.8 wt %) on ZnO NRs samples at a spin speed of 2000 rpm. To enhance the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μl dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solutions were added with 400 μl PEDOT:PSS at 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, 4:4 and 5:4 vol ratios and mixed well by stirring for 5 min. The above solutions (DMSO/PEDOT:PSS) were spin coated on pristine ZnO as well as NGQDs coated ZnO nanorods. All samples were heated at 120 °C for 10 min to remove the solvents. Electrical contacts were made from the bottom FTO electrodes and top PEDOT:PSS polymer using a conducting silver paste (Ted Pella Inc, USA). The active device area for all samples was 0.12 cm2.

The surface topography of PEDOT:PSS films were investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM) in tapping mode (MultiMode 8, Bruker). The electrical properties of PEDOT:PSS and DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS films were investigated using scanning tunneling microscope (STM) (Bruker, MultiMode-8) using a tungsten tip. Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) of the NGQDs were recorded by a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. The scanning electron micrographs (SEM) of the samples were recorded using a Nova Nano (FEI, USA) electron microscope. The UV-VIS-NIR absorption spectra were studied using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. Current-voltage (I-V) characteristics measurements were carried out using a dual channel source meter (B2912A, Agilent, USA). The photodetector properties of the samples were measured under illumination of UV lights from a monochromator attached with a broadband light source (Science Tech, Canada).

To improve the Schottky junction properties and photodetector behaviors of ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS devices, at first the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer was enhanced by adding DMSO solvent. Afterwards, ZnO NRs/polymer Schottky junctions were made with DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS polymer. It has been observed that, the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer can be greatly enhanced by doping with various organic solvents like ethylene glycol (EG), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and sorbitol [48,49] or using strong acid treatment [28,30,50,51]. Although, concentrated acid treatment makes the PEDOT:PSS films highly conductive (∼4100 S/cm) relative to others doping agent [30], but we have observed that, under high acidic medium, ZnO nanorods were fragmented and etched, as a result the device performance was deteriorated. Therefore, in our study, we have optimized the detector photoresponse by doping PEDOT:PSS with various DMSO concentrations.

To increase the electrical conductivity (σ), DMSO solutions were added in PEDOT:PSS with different volume ratios as mentioned in experimental section. The electrical conductivities of the films were measured using four probe method by depositing on glass substrates. The conductivity(σ) of the films with DMSO and PEDOT:PSS volume ratios 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, 4:4 and 5:4 were found to be 19.6, 20.48, 24.0, 19.8 and 16.8 S/cm, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). On the other hand, the conductivity of pristine PEDOT:PSS was estimated to be 1.03 S/cm. In our study, the maximum conductivity was obtained as 24.0 S/cm at volume ratio 3:4. Along with conductivity study, we have also estimated AFM surface roughness of pristine PEDOT:PSS and DMSO modified PEDOT:PSS films (Supporting Information, Fig. S2). Interestingly, the surface of pristine PEDOT:PSS film is quite smooth (Fig. S2a) with rms roughness (Rq) ∼4.52 nm, whereas the DMSO treated films showed higher roughness (Rq ∼25.8 to 52.2 nm). As for example the Rq values for the samples of DMSO and PEDOT:PSS volume ratios 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, 4:4, and 5:4 are found to be 25.8, 29.1, 31.6, 44.5 and 52.2 nm, respectively (Fig. S2(b-f)). The increase of surface roughness with the increasing DMSO concentrations is attributed to the removal of insulating PSS layers and exposed of PEDOT rich grains [20,52]. It has been observed that, PEDOT:PSS polymer is composed of conductive PEDOT rich cores embedded in insulating PSS matrix and the conducting PEDOT-rich grains are separated from each other (Schematic Diagram, Fig. 1 (a)). The π-conjugated polymer chains of PEDOT are responsible for charge conduction. As PEDOT is a water insoluble polymer, a water-soluble polyelectrolyte PSS is usually mixed to improve the water solubility of PEDOT [53]. On the other hand, as PSS insulates the charge transport, we have to compromise with the conductivity of PEDOT. Hence, removing the insulating PSS barrier is crucial to enhance the conductivity of the PEDOT:PSS films. The improvement of conductivity after DMSO treatment can be attributed to intermolecular interactions between DMSO and the sulfonic acid groups of PSS. DMSO interacts with the sulfonic groups of PSS to dissolve the PSS molecules and separates it by weakening the columbic attraction between PEDOT and PSS chains [53]. This causes the PEDOT polymer chains to rearrange themselves which increases the inter-chain interactions and hence the conductivity (Schematic Diagram, Fig. 1(b)). After a optimal doping with DMSO, the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS decrease because of the excess removal of PSS resulting longer average distance between the conductive PEDOT grains [54,55]. This is the reason why the conductivity as well as the device performance was declined after 3:4 vol ratio. We have also studied the STM characterization of PEDOT:PSS and DMSO-PEDOT:PSS films to confirm the improvement of conductivity. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) illustrates the STM current imaging of pristine and DMSO-PEDOT:PSS (3:4 vol ratio) films measured at 0.5V external bias voltage and amplitude set point at 357.79 mV. The bright spots of current imaging can be interpreted as PEDOT rich regions indicating high local conductivities and the darker spots which represent relatively low conductivities are identified as PSS rich areas. Extremely high conductivity over all scan area for DMSO-PEDOT:PSS sample (Fig. 2(b)), compared to pristine PEDOT:PSS (Fig. 2(a)) confirmed the separation of PEDOT chains from the PSS shells after DMSO treatment. Fig. 2(c) shows the typical STM I-V characteristics of pristine PEDOT:PSS films. The inset figures shows the schematic representation of STM study of PEDOT:PSS film and tunneling mechanism under forward and reverse bias. Fig. 2(d) represents the combined STM I-V plots of pristine PEDOT:PSS and DMSO-PEDOT:PSS films. In our STM setup, the bias voltage was applied to the samples keeping tip as ground. Tunneling current in the positive voltage region appeared due to injection of electrons from the tip to the conduction band of PEDOT:PSS. Conversely, the current in the negative bias, occurred due to withdrawal of electrons from valence band of the PEDOT:PSS to the tip. Interestingly, after DMSO doping the tunneling conductance
(
d I / d V
)
increases at both bias voltages (inset of Fig. 2(d)), which is attributed efficient carrier transport in DMSO-PEDOT:PSS films [56].

Fig. 3 (a) represents the top view SEM micrograph of ZnO nanorods grown on FTO coated glass substrate. The nanorods are vertically aligned from the substrate and the average diameter of the nanorods is 100 nm. Fig. 3(b) represent the top view SEM images of the nanorods after deposition of DMSO-PEDOT:PSS (3:4 vol ratio), respectively. The micrographs indicate that, nanorods are covered uniformly by polymer. However, DMSO treated samples showed more transparent nature compared to untreated sample. The photographs of the samples at the inset of Fig. 3(b) demonstrated the transparency of the devices. Fig. 3(c) shows the schematic representation of PEDOT:PSS/ZnO nanorods Schottky junction device. In our present study, we have measured the I-V characteristics of DMSO-PEDOT:PSS/ZnO junctions at various DMSO concentrations. The current density vs. voltage (J-V) characteristics of all samples under dark and illumination of white light of intensity 80 mW/cm2 are presented in Supporting Information, Fig. S3. It is obvious from the results, when we increase DMSO concentration (upto 3:4 vol ratio), the photocurrent (J
light
-J
dark ) at -1V bias of the Schottky junctions increases (Supporting Information, Fig. S3(c)). The photocurrent decreases beyond that above volume ratio (Supporting Information, Fig. S3(d)-(e)). As for example, the photocurrent (J
light
-J
dark ) at -1V bias for pristine PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NRs junction is 1.08 mA/cm2. On the other hand, the photocurrent for the junction with volume ratios 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, 4:4, and 5:4 are 5.88, 17.35, 84.59, 39.40 and 3.70 mA/cm2, respectively. It is obvious that, the maximum photocurrent change occurred with volume ratio 3:4. More importantly, at this ratio, we obtained highest conductivity of the DMSO-PEDOT:PSS films (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). In later discussion, all photodetector measurements were carried out with DMSO-PEDOT:PSS polymer of volume ratio 3:4. To explore, the role of DMSO doping in UV photodetector properties, we have fabricated the following devices. (i) FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOT:PSS (denoted as S1) (ii) FTO/ZnO NRs/DMSO- PEDOT:PSS (denoted as S2) and (iii) FTO/NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/DMSO-PEDOT:PSS (denoted as S3). Fig. 4 (a) shows the AFM topography of hydrothermally grown NGQDs deposited on p-Si substrate. The heights of NGQDs were estimated from AFM topography and found 0.5–3 nm, which indicated that most of the NGQDs consist of 1–3 monolayer grapheme [39]. Fig. 4(b) shows the plane view TEM image of NGQDs with average size ∼ 4.2 nm. High resolution TEM image of a typical NGQD is presented at inset of Fig. 4(b). NGQDs are highly crystalline and the observed lattice distance of 0.34 nm, confirmed the presence of (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [58]. The detail chemical bondings of NGQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron study and reported elsewhere [37]. Fig. 4(c) shows energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) imaging of NGQD decorated ZnO NRs. The elemental mapping of Zn, O, N, C are individually presented in Fig. 4(d). The EDS analysis clearly demonstrated the attachment of NGQDs on ZnO NRs surface.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the J-V characteristics of sample S2 and sample S3, under dark and illumination of white light of intensity 80 mW/cm2. The J-V characteristics of control sample (S1) is presented at the inset. To understand the junction properties of S1, S2 and S3 samples, barrier height (φ) and diode ideality factor (η) were calculated by Cheung and Cheung method [57] (Supporting Information, Table-I). Interestingly, after DMSO modification, the barrier height reduces from 0.65 eV (S1) to 0.46 eV (S2), which is attributed to the decreasing work function of PEDOT:PSS after DMSO doping as reported by Lee et al. [58]. However, for S3 sample, the barrier height has been increased to 0.85eV due to the surface modification with NGQDs. More importantly, the decrease of diode ideality factor (η∼1.7–1.8) after DMSO addition, clearly indicates the improvement of junction properties compared to pristine PEDOT:PSS based device (η∼2.2).

Fig. 5(b) shows the transient photocurrent (J-t) characteristics for S1, S2 and S3 sample under broadband light source of illumination intensity 80 mW/cm2 at −1 V bias voltage. The transient photoresponse of all other samples are shown in supporting information, Fig. S4. Interestingly, the photocurrent change (J
light
-J
dark ) in S1, S2 and S3 samples are 1.9, 166.13 and 188.5 mA/cm2, respectively. Such significant improvement of photocurrent for S2 (87 times higher than S1) and for S3 (99 times higher than S1) is attributed to higher conductivity of PEDOT:PSS electrode, rapid electron-hole collection and better interfacial properties at ZnO NRs/polymer junction. However, slight decrease in transient photocurrent at longer duration of time may be due to the generation of internal heat which decrease hole mobility of PEDOT:PSS under illumination [59].

To obtain the wavelength dependent photoresponse, external quantum efficiency (EQE) has been calculated using following relation [46,60].
(1)
E Q E
( % )
=
1240
J λ
λ
P λ
× 100 %
where, J λ and P
λ are photocurrent density (mA/cm2) and light intensity (mW/cm2) at particular wavelength λ (nm), respectively. The EQE (%) vs. wavelength plots of S1, S2 and S3 samples at -1V bias are presented in Fig. 6 . For all samples, the maximum EQE value was obtained at λ = 340 nm. The optimum vale of EQE for sample S1, S2, and S3 was found to be 1617%, 20615%, and 90063%, respectively. Interestingly, the EQE value increases more than 100% for all samples. Under reverse biased condition, the depletion width at ZnO NRs/polymer Schottky junction reduces due to hole-trapping at the interfaces. After a certain bias, such depletion layer thinning dominates electron tunneling at the junction, which may cause EQE greater than 100% [7]. Interestingly, EQE for S2 sample increases 12 times compared to S1 sample. Such enhancement is attributed to the formation of better Schottky junction due to the modification of PEDOT:PSS polymer. More importantly, the EQE for S3 sample increases 55 times from S1 sample. Such superior EQE for NGQDs modified sample (S3) is attributed to higher UV absorption, efficient carrier transport due to favourable band alignments.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the typical UV–visible absorption spectrum of N-GQDs dispersed in aqueous solution. The absorption spectrum shows two dominant peaks at 237 nm and 334 nm which are ascribed to π→π∗ transition of C=C bonds and the sharp peak at 334 nm corresponds to n→π∗ transition of C=O bonds [37,61]. The broad peak between 460 and 500 nm corresponds to n→π∗ transition in C=N bonds [62]. Fig. 7(b) shows the optical absorption spectra of ZnO NRs, DMSO-PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NRs, NGQDs/ZnO NRs and DMSO-PEDOT:PSS/NGQDs-ZnO NRs samples. Interestingly, the UV absorption enhances after attachment of NGQDs on NR surfaces. More importantly, after PEDOT:PSS coating, the absorption further increases in the wavelength 300–400 nm, which corroborate our EQE results. The superior EQE value in S3 sample is attributed to the photoinduced charge transfer from NGQDs to ZnO NRs under UV light illumination. The schematic representation of the carrier transport mechanism of NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/DMSO-PEDOT:PSS junction Schottky detector is shown at the inset of Fig. 7(b). The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) position of the ZnO NRs, work function of FTO, PEDOT:PSS polymer were obtained from the literature [17,37]. The lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of NGQDs were estimated from cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements [37]. Fig. 8 (a) shows the transient photoresponse of S1, S2 and S3 samples under illumination of UV light of λ∼ 340 nm. All measurements were carried out at -1V bias with incident power 0.38 mW/cm2. The photocurrent change (J
light
-J
dark ) under ON/OFF of UV light has been calculated for the samples S1, S2 and S3 and found to be 2.56, 39.55, and 80.77 mA/cm2, respectively. Interestingly, the photocurrent change in S3 detector is 2 times higher than S2 and 32 times higher than S1. In a Schottky junction photodetector, response and recovery times are very important parameters. We have estimated response and recovery times under turn-on and turn-off of UV light. The rise and decay curves have been fitted by the following equations [63].
(2)
J R
= A
(
1 −
e
−
t
τ 1
)
+ B
(
1 −
e
−
t
τ 2
)
(3)
J D
= A
e
-
t
τ 1
+ B
e
−
t
τ 2
where
J R
and
J D
are the rise and decay photocurrent density, A and B are scaling constant, t is time when UV light was turned on and off, τ 1 and τ 2 are time constants. Fig. 8(b) shows the typical time response of photocurrent growth and decay curves of NGQD sensitized photodetector (S3). In Fig. 8(b), the experimental results are represented by the open circles and the fitted curves are plotted by the solid lines. The transient photocurrent during growth and decay and the corresponding fitted curves for S1 and S2 samples are plotted in supporting information, Fig. S4. The fitted time constants τ 1 and τ 2 during photocurrent growth and decay for all samples are presented in Table 1 . Under illumination, the photocurrent of the detectors increases rapidly. Afterwards, it slows down and reaches a constant value. The existence of two different time constants in ZnO NR based photodetectors can be explained as follows. When the energy of incident photon is greater than the band gap of ZnO (E
g ∼ 3.37 eV) electron-hole pairs are generated. Such band to band transition process is very fast and responsible for quick rise of photocurrent in the first few seconds [11]. On the other hand, the photogenerated holes migrate to the nanorod surface and release the physically adsorbed oxygen which results the decrease of depletion layer thickness and increase the conductivity of the nanorods. This process also increases the photocurrent which is slower compared to initial part. When the light is turned off, the photocurrent decays with faster and slower components. The faster one is attributed to the band-to-band recombination of photogenerated electrons with the holes in ZnO nanorods. On the other hand, surface electrons of NRs are captured by the ambient oxygen molecules by reabsorbing process and increases depletion layer width, which results a slow current decay in photocurrent. The fitted time constants during photocurrent growth for S3 sample are τ 1∼ 0.94 s and τ 2∼2 s. On the other hand, the fast and slow decay time constants are τ 1∼1.9 s and τ 2 ∼25 s, respectively. From the Table 1, it is evident that the rise and decay times are very fast for S2 compared to S1 sample, which is due to the better junction formation and faster carrier transport in DMSO-PEDOT:PSS polymer. More importantly, rapid photocurrent growth and decay in S3 sample is attributed to less surface traps and favourable band alignment at the junction.

To learn the performance of photodetectors, we have also measured the responsivity and the detectivity of the samples at -1V bias voltage. The responsivity (Rλ) and detectivity
(
D λ
)
of the samples has been calculated using the following relation [46,60].
(5)
R λ
=
J
l i g h t
−
J
d a r k
P
o p t
(6)
D λ
=
R λ
2 q
J
d a r k
where J
light and J
dark are the current density at light and dark, respectively. P
opt is the intensity of incident light at a particular wavelength λ and q is the electronic charge. Fig. 9 (a) and (b) represent the responsivity and detectivity of the samples, respectively. Similar to EQE, the responsivity and detectivity for all samples have been found to be maximum at wavelength 340 nm. The maximum responsivity for S1, S2 and S3 samples are found to be 4.43, 56.52 and 247 A/W, respectively. On the other hand, the maximum detectivity for S1, S2 and S3 samples are 1.23 × 1010, 1.07 × 1011 and 2.42 × 1011 Hz1/2/W, respectively.

The UV sensing performances of already reported various ZnO/PEDOT:PSS structure based devices are compared in the following Table 2 . To the best of our knowledge, in comparison with the available literatures, the proposed device showed superior performances in terms of photocurrent and responsivity.

In summary, we have reported the fabrication of easy, low cost, solution processed UV detectors using ZnO nanorods/PEDOT:PSS polymer Schottky junction. The device performance was monitored by improving the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS polymer using DMSO additive. The improvement of the conductivity of DMSO-PEDOT:PSS polymer was studied using four probe resistivity along with STM measurements. The optimum device performance and photoresponse properties were obtained with DMSO-PEDOT:PSS (3:4 vol ratio) sample of conductivity 20S/cm. The photodetector properties was further enhanced by sensitizing ZnO nanorods with N doped GQDs. NGQD decorated ZnO NRs/DMSO-PEDOT:PSS Schottky junction device (sample S3) demonstrated maximum EQE 90063% at λ = 340 nm, which was 56 times higher than pristine PEDOT:PSS/ZnO NR based device (sample S1). Sample S3 also showed higher photoresponse behaviour under ON/OFF of UV light (λ∼ 340 nm) compared to other samples. The photocurrent change (J
light
-J
dark ) for the samples S1, S2 and S3 were found to be 2.56, 39.55, and 80.77 mA/cm2, respectively. The photocurrent rise and decay times of NGQD sensitized NRs were very fast compared to S1 and S2 samples. Our UV detector also showed high responsivity (∼247 A/W) and detectivity (∼2.42 × 1011 Hz1/2/W) at λ∼ 340 nm. This present study demonstrated the potential use of semiconductor/polymer Schottky junction for ultraviolet photodetector applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Both authors Saurab Dhar and Tanmoy Majumder contributed equally to this work. This present research work was partially funded by CSIR Extramural Research Grant, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledged the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for UV-VIS-NIR spectroscopy, XRD, AFM characterizations.
